result: I keep thinking about the small pause between a lightning flash and the sound that follows, how that sliver of silence feels almost edible, like you could bite it and taste something metallic and ancient. The sky keeps a stopwatch for us, though we rarely check it, choosing instead to argue about the shape of clouds. My neighbor swears the maple by the mailbox is dying; I think it’s just bored, practicing choreography for a slow-motion ballet no one bought tickets to. Either way, its roots are drinking secrets from the soil—last year’s dog bones, the funeral programs that blew away, a child’s tooth lost during hide-and-seek. At night the tree whispers statistics about gravity, how ninety percent of falling is actually floating upward in reverse. I believe it, the same way I believe unread books grow heavier, ballast against the drift of forgetting. Sometimes I open windows on both sides of the house to let the breeze clean out the sentences I never said; they scatter across the yard like loose change, glinting when the streetlamp flickers on. One coin always rolls under the azalea, waiting for the moment I finally need those words back. By then the azalea will have blossomed, dropped, and re-blossomed, practicing its own quiet patience. Somewhere inside every repeated year is a single new second—unlabeled, unclaimed—where the lightning and thunder happen at once, and nobody notices the sky has finally caught up with itself.
